banner: Week 34 · The Six Trig Ratios — Reciprocals Complete the Family
essential_question: Sine, cosine, and tangent each compare two sides of a right triangle — what happens if you just flip the fraction?
objective: Define cosecant, secant, and cotangent as the reciprocals of sine, cosine, and tangent, and compute all six trigonometric ratios (reduced) for a given right triangle.

content.prompt: sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse. If you flip that fraction upside down — hypotenuse/opposite — is the new number still telling you something true about the triangle? What would you call it?

agenda.title: I do — Three Ratios, Three Reciprocals

content.heading: SOHCAHTOA's three missing partners
bullets: sin θ = opp/hyp, and its reciprocal is csc θ (cosecant) = hyp/opp.
cos θ = adj/hyp, and its reciprocal is sec θ (secant) = hyp/adj.
tan θ = opp/adj, and its reciprocal is cot θ (cotangent) = adj/opp.
Reciprocal means 'flip the fraction' — the same two sides, swapped top and bottom. Multiply any ratio by its reciprocal and you always get 1.
Always reduce every ratio to lowest terms, exactly like Math-U-See asks on Lesson Practice 30A/30B.

agenda.title: Explore — Drag θ, Watch All Six Update

content.heading: One triangle, six live ratios
content.note: On the sim, drag the gold point to change θ. Watch all six ratios update as fractions of the current side lengths AND as decimals. Try the 30°, 45°, and 60° preset buttons and compare to what you already know from Lessons 20-21. Confirm sin θ × csc θ = 1 (and the other two reciprocal pairs) at every angle you try.

content.preview: Build session: measure a real right triangle end to end and use trig to solve a real height/distance problem.

success_criteria.text: I can define cosecant, secant, and cotangent as the reciprocals of sine, cosine, and tangent
I can compute all six trigonometric ratios, reduced, for a given right triangle
I can state and verify that sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 for any right triangle

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W34 The Six Trig Ratios.md

questions.prompt: The reciprocal of sin θ is called…
questions.explain: csc θ (cosecant) = hyp/opp, exactly sin θ = opp/hyp flipped upside down.
cot θ (cotangent) is the reciprocal of tan θ = opp/adj, so cot θ = adj/opp.

questions.prompt: A right triangle has legs 6 and 8 and hypotenuse 10. θ sits at the vertex between the leg of 8 and the hypotenuse, so the leg of 6 is opposite θ and the leg of 8 is adjacent to θ. Find sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ. Reduce every fraction.
questions.explain: Opposite (6) over hypotenuse (10) gives sin; adjacent (8) over hypotenuse (10) gives cos; opposite (6) over adjacent (8) gives tan. Each reduces by dividing by 2.

questions.prompt: Using the same triangle (legs 6 and 8, hypotenuse 10, opposite = 6, adjacent = 8), find csc θ, sec θ, and cot θ by flipping your answers from the last question. Reduce every fraction.
questions.explain: Each reciprocal ratio is the matching ratio from the previous question flipped upside down: csc flips sin, sec flips cos, cot flips tan.

questions.prompt: On the grid, sketch a right triangle with legs 6 and 8 and hypotenuse 10. Mark θ at one acute vertex and label which leg is opposite θ and which is adjacent to θ.
questions.answer: A right triangle with legs 6 and 8, hypotenuse 10, θ marked at one acute vertex, the leg across from θ labeled 'opposite,' and the leg touching θ (not the hypotenuse) labeled 'adjacent.'
questions.explain: Rubric: a right triangle with the correct side lengths (1 pt) + opposite/adjacent correctly labeled relative to θ (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Fill in the blank, then explain in one sentence why it must be true for every right triangle: sin²θ + cos²θ = ______.
questions.answer: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1. It must be true because sin θ = opp/hyp and cos θ = adj/hyp, so sin²θ + cos²θ = (opp² + adj²)/hyp² = hyp²/hyp² = 1 by the Pythagorean theorem (opp² + adj² = hyp²).
questions.explain: This is the Pythagorean theorem divided through by hyp² — it holds for any right triangle, not just this one, since a² + b² = c² is always true.

questions.prompt: Exit: pick one of the three reciprocal pairs (sin/csc, cos/sec, tan/cot) and explain, in your own words, why multiplying a ratio by its reciprocal always gives exactly 1.
